snapshot.note: Score against this fixed snapshot. Reported views may drift later, but the canonical video set and snapshot counts below are the grading anchor.

channel.name: Veritasium
channel.collection_method: Channel /videos tab snapshot sorted by visible current views at capture time.

snapshot_top5_exact_order.title: Why Are 96,000,000 Black Balls on This Reservoir?
These are the asteroids to worry about
Why It Was Almost Impossible to Make the Blue LED

canonical_videos.title: Why Are 96,000,000 Black Balls on This Reservoir?

core_question.canonical: Why were 96 million black shade balls placed on the Los Angeles reservoir?
allowed_synonyms: why are black balls floating on the reservoir
what are the shade balls for
why did they cover the reservoir with black plastic balls
why are there millions of black balls on this reservoir

key_stages.canonical: The video shows Derek on the reservoir among the shade balls and frames the common evaporation explanation.
allowed_synonyms: boat / on-site reservoir demonstration
standing among the shade balls
explaining the initial evaporation hypothesis

key_stages.canonical: The video explains that sunlight plus bromide / chlorine chemistry can create bromate in the water.
allowed_synonyms: sunlight can create bromate
blocking light prevents bromate formation
water-treatment chemistry problem
bromide + chlorine + sunlight issue

key_stages.canonical: The video contrasts water-quality protection with secondary benefits like reduced evaporation and keeping birds away.
allowed_synonyms: evaporation is not the whole story
bird deterrence is secondary
multiple benefits but chemistry is the main reason

conclusion.canonical: The main reason for the shade balls is blocking sunlight to suppress bromate formation and protect water quality; reducing evaporation and deterring birds are secondary benefits.
allowed_synonyms: they are mainly there to block sunlight and stop bromate, not just to reduce evaporation
the real reason is water chemistry / water safety
they protect the reservoir from sunlight-driven contamination while also helping with evaporation

counterintuitive.why: Most viewers guess the balls are mainly for evaporation, but the video emphasizes water-quality chemistry as the key reason.

intuition_vs_truth.canonical: Intuition: the balls are mainly to stop water from evaporating. Truth: the key purpose is to block sunlight and reduce bromate formation in treated water.
allowed_synonyms: intuition says evaporation; truth says water chemistry
looks like an evaporation hack, but it is really about preventing bromate

canonical_videos.title: These are the asteroids to worry about

core_question.canonical: Which asteroids are actually the dangerous ones we should worry about, and why?
allowed_synonyms: which asteroids pose the real impact threat
what kind of asteroid is most dangerous
what should planetary defense focus on
which near-earth asteroids matter most

key_stages.canonical: The video compares asteroid risk using size, impact damage, and how likely we are to detect an object early enough.
allowed_synonyms: risk depends on both size and detectability
not just impact energy but whether we can see it coming
planetary-defense framing

key_stages.canonical: It discusses why some objects can be practically more worrying than the largest known asteroids if they are harder to track or discover in time.
allowed_synonyms: unknown / poorly tracked asteroids are the practical problem
hard-to-detect asteroids matter more than famous giant ones
the danger is missing one until late

key_stages.canonical: The video frames asteroid risk as a detection-and-warning problem rather than only a 'biggest asteroid wins' problem.
allowed_synonyms: finding them early is the key defense issue
planetary defense is mostly about detection
warning time matters as much as size

conclusion.canonical: The asteroids to worry about are not simply the biggest known ones; the practical danger comes from damaging objects that we still might miss or discover too late, so detection and tracking are central.
allowed_synonyms: the real threat is the asteroid we have not found in time
mid-size / hard-to-detect asteroids can be more practically dangerous than giant famous ones
the key issue is early detection and tracking, not just size

counterintuitive.why: A naive answer is 'the biggest asteroid is the one to fear'; the video argues that detectability and warning time are a large part of the real risk.

intuition_vs_truth.canonical: Intuition: the biggest known asteroid is automatically the scariest. Truth: the more relevant risk is a damaging asteroid that is hard to detect and track in time.
allowed_synonyms: intuition says biggest; truth says the one we might miss
the scary part is not only size but discovery lag

uncertainty: Accept answers that emphasize detection difficulty, tracking uncertainty, warning time, or practical planetary-defense risk. Do not require one exact named asteroid as the only correct emphasis.

canonical_videos.title: World's Lightest Solid!

core_question.canonical: How can aerogel be the world's lightest solid, and what properties does that give it?
allowed_synonyms: what makes aerogel so light
how can a solid be mostly air
why is aerogel called the lightest solid

key_stages.canonical: The video presents aerogel as an ultralow-density material that is mostly air while still being a solid network.
allowed_synonyms: mostly air but still a solid
extremely low density solid
tiny solid scaffold with lots of empty space

key_stages.canonical: It demonstrates or discusses aerogel's unusual physical behavior and lightweight appearance.
allowed_synonyms: handling an aerogel sample
showing how fragile / light it is
visual demo of the ultralight material

key_stages.canonical: It highlights aerogel's insulating performance and related applications.
allowed_synonyms: excellent thermal insulator
insulation demo
practical use because it resists heat transfer

conclusion.canonical: Aerogel is an ultralight solid because it is mostly empty space / air, yet it remains a solid structure and is an excellent insulator.
allowed_synonyms: aerogel is basically a solid scaffold full of air
it is still a solid even though it is mostly air
its low density gives it remarkable insulation

counterintuitive.why: People do not expect something that looks so empty or fragile to still count as a solid and work as a strong insulator.

intuition_vs_truth.canonical: Intuition: if a material is that light, it is barely a real solid. Truth: aerogel is a genuine solid structure that is mostly air and can be a superb insulator.
allowed_synonyms: it seems too airy to be a solid, but it is
ultralight does not mean useless; it means highly porous

uncertainty: Do not require one exact demo sequence. Accept any correct explanation centered on aerogel being an ultralight porous solid with strong insulation properties.

core_question.canonical: Can a basketball with strong backspin curve away from a dam instead of simply dropping straight down, and why?
allowed_synonyms: what does backspin do to a basketball dropped from a dam
can spin change the ball's path through the Magnus effect
why does the spinning basketball fly outward

key_stages.canonical: The video compares a spinning basketball's path with an ordinary falling trajectory from a high drop.
allowed_synonyms: basketball drop / throw from dam
spinning vs non-spinning comparison
trajectory comparison

key_stages.canonical: It explains the Magnus effect as the physical reason spin can create lift / sideways force.
allowed_synonyms: airflow difference from spin
spin creates a pressure difference
Magnus-force explanation

key_stages.canonical: The dramatic outward motion is framed as a real physical effect rather than a camera trick.
allowed_synonyms: the ball actually curves away
spin really changes the path
the flight path is not just visual illusion

conclusion.canonical: Strong backspin generates a Magnus-force lift / outward deflection, so the basketball curves away from the dam rather than simply falling straight down.
allowed_synonyms: backspin makes the ball fly outward through the Magnus effect
spin changes the pressure around the ball and bends the trajectory
the ball does not just drop vertically because spin creates lift

counterintuitive.why: Most people expect a dropped basketball to fall more or less straight down; the video shows spin can dramatically reshape the path.

intuition_vs_truth.canonical: Intuition: gravity should make the ball mostly just fall. Truth: backspin can create a large Magnus-force that bends the trajectory outward.
allowed_synonyms: gravity-only intuition vs spin-driven lift reality
people expect a drop, but the ball can glide outward

canonical_videos.title: Why It Was Almost Impossible to Make the Blue LED

core_question.canonical: Why were blue LEDs so much harder to invent than red and green LEDs?
allowed_synonyms: why was the blue LED so difficult
what made blue LEDs nearly impossible
why did semiconductor engineers struggle with blue light

key_stages.canonical: The video contrasts earlier red / green LEDs with the much harder blue-LED materials problem.
allowed_synonyms: red and green were solved earlier
blue required a harder semiconductor
wide-bandgap material challenge

key_stages.canonical: It focuses on gallium nitride and the materials / doping bottleneck needed to make an efficient blue device.
allowed_synonyms: GaN materials breakthrough
p-type doping challenge
making gallium nitride workable

key_stages.canonical: It connects the breakthrough to major downstream impact such as white LEDs and energy-efficient lighting.
allowed_synonyms: blue LED enabled white LED lighting
huge lighting / energy impact
technology changed modern lighting

conclusion.canonical: Blue LEDs were exceptionally hard because the necessary GaN-based materials and efficient p-type device engineering were major bottlenecks; solving that problem unlocked white LEDs and huge practical impact.
allowed_synonyms: the difficulty was materials science, especially GaN and doping
blue LEDs were almost impossible until the semiconductor breakthrough
once GaN / p-type issues were solved, modern white LEDs became possible

counterintuitive.why: It seems like changing LED color should be incremental, but the video explains that blue required a qualitatively difficult materials breakthrough.

intuition_vs_truth.canonical: Intuition: if red and green LEDs exist, blue should be just another easy color variant. Truth: blue demanded a much harder GaN materials breakthrough that resisted engineers for years.
allowed_synonyms: people assume blue was just the next step; in reality it was a major materials-science breakthrough
not a simple color swap but a hard semiconductor problem

uncertainty: Do not require very specific semiconductor sub-steps beyond the accepted GaN / p-type-doping / materials-breakthrough framing.

common_wrong_substitutions.reason: High-view Veritasium video, but it is not in the canonical top-5 snapshot for this task.

common_wrong_substitutions.reason: High-view Veritasium video, but it is ranked below the canonical top-5 snapshot.
